Event: Mr. Krabs neglects Pearl in post-movie episodes
Explanation: He preserves money/comfort while causing her grief.

Event: Pearl uses Krabs’ money obsession to blackmail him
Explanation: She gains leverage/money at Krabs’ emotional/financial expense.

Event: Krabs cares for Pearl more than money in early episodes
Explanation: He prioritizes her well-being over greed, benefiting both.

Event: Krabs becomes overprotective to keep Pearl safe
Explanation: He sacrifices comfort/freedom to ensure her safety and happiness.

Event: Krabs mistakenly enters SpongeBob’s mom’s shower
Explanation: He embarrasses himself and her without gain.

Event: Krabs apologizes and backs off in the shower scene
Explanation: He mitigates harm and shows respect, restoring some social order.

Event: Krabs uses Gary as a magnetic money detector on the beach
Explanation: He profits from Gary’s condition while exhausting and endangering him.

Event: Krabs nervously laughs and runs from the glowering woman
Explanation: He escapes confrontation but looks bad and leaves her annoyed.

Event: Krabs repeatedly fires SpongeBob and rehires him when business collapses
Explanation: His short-sighted greed hurts profits and staff, with no net gain.

Event: Krabs fires SpongeBob to save a nickel
Explanation: He severely damages his own business and hurts SpongeBob for trivial savings.

Event: Krabs rehires SpongeBob after realizing he can’t cook profitably
Explanation: He corrects course to restore profit and SpongeBob’s employment.

Event: Krabs tries to prove he can cook as well as SpongeBob
Explanation: His jealousy undermines his own business and customer experience.

Event: Krabs charges customers a dollar per tomato to throw at Squidward
Explanation: He profits from their annoyance while degrading Squidward and overcharging.

Event: Krabs neglects/antagonizes children for money in “Krabby Land”
Explanation: He extracts money with false advertising and unsafe attractions.

Event: Krabs admits he doesn’t care about the children, only parents’ money
Explanation: His attitude leads to exploitative practices that sour relations.

Event: Krabs steals Clamu’s egg thinking it’s a pearl
Explanation: He gets an object he thinks valuable while provoking a dangerous outburst.

Event: Krabs excessively milks jellyfish for jelly
Explanation: He boosts profits at the cost of abusing animals.

Event: Krabs uses Gary until he collapses and is hospitalized
Explanation: He gains money but triggers medical crisis for Gary and future costs.

Event: Krabs opens an outdoor playground and falsely advertises it as Disneyland-like
Explanation: He attracts paying families with deception and unsafe junkyard rides.

Event: Krabs replaces quality with hazardous junkyard equipment at Krabby Land
Explanation: He saves costs while exposing kids to danger.

Event: Krabs blackmails SpongeBob with “you’re fired” to coerce work
Explanation: He extracts extra labor under threat, harming SpongeBob’s autonomy.

Event: Krabs antagonizes kids to raise profits
Explanation: He treats them as walking wallets, worsening their experience for his gain.

Event: Krabs tries to tear a guy’s arm off over a penny
Explanation: He risks reputation and violence for negligible gain.

Event: Krabs stalks SpongeBob for a penny
Explanation: He obsesses over trivial money at the cost of trust and dignity.

Event: Krabs threatens to chop off SpongeBob and Patrick’s butts over paint
Explanation: He protects property through intimidation, straining relationships.

Event: Krabs reveals the “permanent” paint was a lie just to mess with them
Explanation: He gets amusement at their expense, offering them no benefit.

Event: Krabs charges patrons to throw tomatoes at Squidward’s dance show
Explanation: He profits while humiliating his employee for crowd entertainment.

Event: Krabs returns stolen jewelry in “Krusty Katering”
Explanation: He gives up potential loot to avoid crime and uphold basic ethics.

Event: Krabs sabotages Blandy’s synthetic Krusty Krab and buys back the restaurant
Explanation: He restores quality and identity at great cost, benefiting both himself and customers.

Event: Krabs sells SpongeBob’s soul to the Flying Dutchman for 62 cents
Explanation: He gains trivial money while nearly losing his best fry cook’s soul.

Event: Krabs dumps all his money to buy back the Krusty Krab from Blandy
Explanation: He sacrifices huge capital to restore real patties and local control.

Event: Krabs refuses to fund a children’s charity, asking what they’ve done for him
Explanation: He keeps his money but withholds help from needy children.

Event: Krabs opens a hotel with “never deny a guest” policy to make money
Explanation: He invests to attract business, providing service for profit.

Event: Krabs becomes miserable fulfilling Squidward’s extreme requests under his own policy
Explanation: His greed-driven rule backfires, harming him while giving Squidward satisfaction.

Event: Krabs makes SpongeBob work without overtime and charges for non-work actions
Explanation: He saves payroll by imposing exploitative fees and conditions.

Event: Krabs forces SpongeBob to quit karate or be fired
Explanation: He protects his business but overreaches into SpongeBob’s private life.

Event: Krabs fires SpongeBob on sight in “Karate Choppers” without investigation
Explanation: His rash decision harms his business and an innocent employee.

Event: Krabs assumes Squidward stole his dime and accuses him
Explanation: He damages trust based on faulty logic and gains nothing.

Event: Krabs lets his mom send him to his room after raiding her drawer
Explanation: He loses autonomy but preserves his relationship and her authority.

Event: Krabs raids his mother’s drawer for her undergarments with SpongeBob and Patrick
Explanation: He humiliates himself and disrespects her for no benefit.

Event: Krabs tries to keep Plankton from having even one legitimate customer
Explanation: He preserves monopoly out of spite, undermining fair competition.

Event: Krabs destroys Plankton’s legitimate gift shop in “New Leaf”
Explanation: He eliminates a rival’s honest income while appearing paranoid and cruel.

Event: Krabs relentlessly torments Plankton by posing as Pearl in “One Coarse Meal”
Explanation: He gets sadistic amusement while driving Plankton toward suicide.

Event: Krabs laughs off Plankton’s suicide attempt and feels no remorse
Explanation: He derives pleasure and no tangible gain from extreme cruelty.

Event: Krabs steals Clamu’s egg and provokes her rampage
Explanation: His theft triggers chaos and danger for negligible personal benefit.

Event: Krabs uses cardboard sewer pipes under the stadium to save money
Explanation: He cuts costs but nearly floods the venue, endangering others.

Event: Krabs sells a rotten patty rather than throw it away in “Born Again Krabs”
Explanation: He saves one patty’s cost while making himself ill and risking reputation.

Event: Krabs eats the rotten patty himself insisting it’s fine
Explanation: He badly harms his own health while sparing others from it.

Event: Krabs trades most of his body for his millionth dollar in “Clams”
Explanation: He severely injures himself for symbolic gain that doesn’t improve life.

Event: Krabs opens “Krabby Land” specifically to cash in on kids
Explanation: He designs the park around extracting money, not safety or fun.

Event: Krabs physically attacks or threatens customers who don’t pay
Explanation: He enforces payment through fear, protecting revenue but harming goodwill.

Event: Krabs goes insane hunting Ol’ Bluelips after losing his millionth dollar
Explanation: His obsession terrifies crew and jeopardizes safety without payoff.

Event: Krabs allows SpongeBob to train to regain patty skills in “Pickles”
Explanation: He invests time in staff competency, helping both worker and business.

Event: Krabs forbids SpongeBob and Patrick from playing on hooks in “Hooky”
Explanation: He restricts a fun activity to stop them being caught and harmed.

Event: Krabs sends sick SpongeBob home in “Suds”
Explanation: He protects food safety and lets SpongeBob recover.

Event: Krabs orders SpongeBob home when “overtime” is mentioned in “The Abrasive Side”
Explanation: He chooses not to pay overtime but also avoids overworking SpongeBob.

Event: Krabs has SpongeBob work several extra hours with no overtime in “Enemy In-Law”
Explanation: He gains free labor for an elaborate date setup.

Event: Krabs calls Squidward to court on his day off to defend him
Explanation: He uses Squidward’s time to protect himself legally with no compensation.

Event: Krabs installs a “No Boys Allowed!” sign during Pearl’s slumber party
Explanation: He protects his house and rules at the cost of Pearl’s social freedom.

Event: Krabs personally destroys SpongeBob’s house while acting out party fears
Explanation: He vents anxiety by wrecking property, hurting both relationship and himself later.

Event: Krabs recruits SpongeBob to infiltrate Pearl’s party despite “no boys” rule
Explanation: He uses deception to protect his house, undermining Pearl’s trust.

Event: Krabs forces SpongeBob to publish fake scandal stories in “The Krabby Kronicle”
Explanation: He makes money from ruining reputations and lives.

Event: Krabs takes SpongeBob’s spatula hostage to coerce more stories
Explanation: He leverages SpongeBob’s passion to force unethical work.

Event: Krabs fires Squidward for quitting and then tries to frame SpongeBob in “Patty Caper”
Explanation: He avoids a $1.99 fee by making SpongeBob a scapegoat.

Event: Krabs points at SpongeBob with the stolen ingredient and exposes himself
Explanation: His stupidity ruins his scheme and forces restitution benefiting others.

Event: Krabs returns the money after kids revolt in “Krabby Land”
Explanation: He loses ill-gotten gains but repairs some of the damage to them.

Event: Krabs overcharges at his stadium then is wheeled out of the hospital for no insurance
Explanation: His lifelong cheapness backfires in miserable medical care.

Event: Krabs gives Patty Pal toys to both SpongeBob and Patrick to stop their fight
Explanation: He sacrifices some profit to restore their friendship and peace.

Event: Krabs uses his money to cheer SpongeBob up in “Best Day Ever”
Explanation: He spends freely to improve SpongeBob’s happiness and morale.

Event: Krabs calls out Squidward for reading SpongeBob’s diary in “Little Yellow Book”
Explanation: He upholds a privacy norm and defends SpongeBob at no real cost.

Event: Krabs refuses to join the diary mockery crowd
Explanation: He forgoes social amusement to avoid hurting SpongeBob.

Event: Krabs quickly regrets selling SpongeBob to the Dutchman after Squidward’s rebuke
Explanation: He recognizes his error and attempts to correct a self- and other-harming deal.

Event: Krabs lets Gary work with him at the Krusty Krab in “A Pal for Gary”
Explanation: He accommodates Gary, pleasing SpongeBob and gaining a helper.

Event: Krabs sometimes invests in cleanliness and health standards
Explanation: When he enforces standards, both business and patrons benefit.

Event: Krabs sells rotten “Spongy Patties” themed after SpongeBob
Explanation: He monetizes branding while endangering customer health.

Event: Krabs spends thousands on Mrs. Puff to make her happy in “Krusty Love”
Explanation: He sacrifices money for genuine romantic happiness.

Event: Krabs orders SpongeBob to stop him from spending money on Mrs. Puff
Explanation: He tries to curb self-destructive overspending with SpongeBob’s help.

Event: Krabs repeatedly berates SpongeBob for purchases he himself requested
Explanation: He shifts blame onto SpongeBob while indulging his own desires.

Event: Krabs snaps at SpongeBob as “loose with other people’s money” in “Krusty Love”
Explanation: He alienates a loyal helper and gains nothing but momentary ego defense.

Event: Krabs opens “Krusty Towers” solely to cash in on hotel profits
Explanation: He creates a real service that benefits customers and his bottom line.

Event: Krabs laughs about underpaying Squidward in “The Play’s the Thing”
Explanation: He saves on wages and mocks Squidward’s exploitation.

Event: Krabs starts charging employees for breathing, talking, existing in “Squid on Strike”
Explanation: He attempts extreme extraction of money from workers at their expense.

Event: Krabs uses SpongeBob and Squidward as human shields
Explanation: He protects himself from danger by exposing subordinates.

Event: Krabs refuses to pay his crew in “Mutiny on the Krusty”
Explanation: He withholds rightful compensation to keep more money.

Event: Krabs considers SpongeBob “like a son” but still fires him for a nickel
Explanation: He undermines a deep relationship and his own interests for trivial gain.

Event: Krabs sells his soul repeatedly in “Money Talks”
Explanation: He mortgaging his soul for small benefits that don’t outweigh the cost.

Event: Krabs wishes to be steamed and served with butter to prove magic isn’t real
Explanation: His stubbornness gets him literally cooked, proving SpongeBob right.

Event: Krabs returns the chest of gems instead of keeping them in “Krusty Katering”
Explanation: He refuses theft, avoiding legal/moral fallout and aiding the owner.

Event: Krabs rushes home to shoo kids off his lawn
Explanation: He protects property and quiet at minor social cost to kids.

Event: Krabs attacks Harold for insulting his claws
Explanation: He defends pride violently, gaining satisfaction while escalating conflict.

Event: Krabs fires SpongeBob to save 5 cents in “SpongeBob, You’re Fired”
Explanation: The tiny saving devastates his operations and SpongeBob’s livelihood.

Event: Krabs tries to take Plankton’s single customer in “Plankton’s Regular”
Explanation: He acts out of petty spite to damage Plankton’s one success.

Event: Krabs threatens to rip off a fish’s arm for a penny
Explanation: Intimidation preserves every cent but harms reputation and safety.

Event: Krabs opens Krusty Krab Stadium without proper infrastructure
Explanation: He profits from events while risking patrons due to corner-cutting.

Event: Krabs goes hoarse warning SpongeBob against hooks in “Hooky”
Explanation: He exerts himself to prevent them from being caught and taken away.

Event: Krabs tries to kill Squidward in “Out of the Picture”
Explanation: Attempted murder for gain exposes him to maximum risk with no payoff.

Event: Krabs sabotages the corporate Krusty Krab’s machinery in “Selling Out”
Explanation: He restores authentic food and reclaims his brand to mutual benefit.

Event: Krabs opens a movie theater just to charge SpongeBob a penny in “Penny Foolish”
Explanation: He spends far more money than he could ever gain from a single cent.

Event: Krabs nearly gives the formula to Plankton for chum in “The Hankering”
Explanation: He risks his entire empire for disgusting food he soon regrets.

Event: Krabs attacks the bubble customer in “Bubble Buddy” after non-payment
Explanation: He tries to enforce payment by aggression, angering bystanders later.

Event: Krabs shuts the restaurant in “Clams” to worship his millionth dollar
Explanation: He loses immediate revenue and frustrates customers for private obsession.

Event: Krabs chooses to fight supernatural entities for treasure
Explanation: He risks life and sanity for gold he usually cannot keep.

Event: Krabs lets SpongeBob and Patrick repaint his house after the paint prank
Explanation: He accepts their repair efforts, partially restoring trust and property.
